topic: A student is accused of plagiarism
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the library hummed, a grating soundtrack to my internal turmoil. The accusation, delivered in the hushed, sterile environment of the department office, felt like a physical blow. My hands, gripping the edge of the worn textbook, were slick with a cold sweat. Every glance from a passing student felt like a spotlight, dissecting the inadequacy of my work. I kept my gaze fixed on the dust motes dancing in the beam of light, refusing to meet anyone’s eyes. When the professor finally spoke, his voice was level, devoid of anger, which somehow made the judgment feel heavier. "The similarities are undeniable," he stated, and the silence that followed stretched taut, vibrating with my failure. I could only manage a small, jerky nod, my throat too tight to form a coherent defense.

[story 2]

I walked across the quad feeling like a ghost haunting my own existence. The sunlight, usually a source of warmth, felt harsh, exposing every flaw in my composure. My backpack straps dug into my shoulders, a constant, uncomfortable pressure mirroring the knot tightening in my chest. Every casual greeting from a classmate felt like an accusation, a silent acknowledgment of the stain on my record. I kept my head down, focusing intently on the cracked pavement beneath my sneakers, trying to merge with the concrete. When I finally reached my dorm room, the door clicked shut, and I leaned against it, sliding down until I was sitting on the floor. The silence was deafening, punctuated only by the frantic rhythm of my own pulse hammering against my ribs.

[story 3]

The words swam before my eyes as I reread the formal notification. The paper felt flimsy, easily torn, and I instinctively curled my fingers around the edges, as if to physically contain the unwelcome truth. I started pacing the small confines of my apartment, the floorboards protesting softly beneath my hurried steps. Each footfall seemed to echo the hollow sound of my own inadequacy. I caught my reflection in the darkened windowpane—a pale, drawn face staring back, eyes wide and unseeing. A sudden wave of nausea washed over me, making my stomach clench painfully. I stopped pacing abruptly, pressing my palms against my temples, trying to physically push the swirling uncertainty out of my mind.
